questions.question: Why can you not directly read the congestion window (cwnd) from a packet capture?
options: cwnd is encrypted inside the TCP payload
cwnd is internal sender state and never appears on the wire
Wireshark does not support TCP analysis
cwnd is only in IPv6 packets
questions.explanation: The congestion window is a sender-side variable. It must be inferred from the number of unacknowledged bytes in flight per RTT.

questions.question: What evidence in a trace indicates a fast retransmit event in TCP Reno?
options: A single duplicate ACK
Three duplicate ACKs acknowledging the same sequence number
An ICMP source quench message
A window advertisement of zero
questions.explanation: Reno triggers fast retransmit after receiving three duplicate ACKs for the same sequence number, indicating a segment was lost.

questions.question: After a timeout-based loss, what happens to cwnd and ssthresh?
options: cwnd stays the same, ssthresh doubles
cwnd resets to 1 MSS, ssthresh is set to half the previous flight size
Both cwnd and ssthresh are set to zero
cwnd doubles, ssthresh stays the same
questions.explanation: On timeout, Reno sets ssthresh to half the current flight size and resets cwnd to 1 MSS, restarting slow-start.

questions.question: How do you distinguish slow-start from congestion avoidance when reconstructing cwnd from a trace?
options: Slow start has linear growth; congestion avoidance has exponential growth
Slow start has exponential cwnd growth per RTT; congestion avoidance has linear growth of 1 MSS per RTT
They are identical phases with different names
Slow start only occurs after loss
questions.explanation: During slow start, cwnd doubles every RTT (exponential). In congestion avoidance, cwnd increases by approximately 1 MSS per RTT (linear).

questions.question: What is bytes-in-flight and why is it central to cwnd reconstruction?
options: Bytes waiting in the send buffer; it limits throughput
The difference between highest sequence sent and highest ack received; it approximates cwnd when sampled at its peak per RTT
The TCP window advertisement; it sets the receiver limit
The number of retransmitted segments; it indicates loss
questions.explanation: Bytes-in-flight = highest_seq_sent - highest_ack_received. Its peak within an RTT window approximates the effective cwnd, bounded by the receiver advertised window.
